content.text: What it is

Operational handoff requirements define what a PoC team must deliver to a platform or operations team before responsibility for an agentic AI feature transitions to production operations. In most enterprises, the team that builds a PoC is not the team that operates the production system — different skills, different incentives, and different time horizons. Without a structured handoff, the production operations team inherits a system they do not understand, with undocumented failure modes, a prompt that was never version-controlled, cost models based on PoC-scale assumptions, and no established change control process for AI-specific artifacts like system prompts and evaluation suites. The handoff requirements close this gap by specifying exactly what knowledge and documentation the PoC team must transfer.
Why it matters

The most common source of preventable production incidents in enterprise AI programs is not technical failure — it is knowledge failure: the operators who receive the system do not understand its AI-specific failure modes, do not know how to change the system prompt safely, and do not have the context to interpret quality degradation signals. The PoC team, pressed for time and often dissolved or redeployed after launch, transfers this knowledge informally or not at all. Operational handoff requirements make the transfer explicit, checkboxed, and required before the PoC team's responsibility ends.
Benefits
Knowledge preservation: the system's design rationale, known limitations, and failure modes are documented and transferred rather than lost when the PoC team moves on.
Faster incident response: operators who received a complete handoff can diagnose and remediate AI-specific failures in minutes rather than hours, because they have runbooks and escalation paths.
Safe prompt changes: the prompt change control process in the handoff ensures that production system prompts are changed safely, with evaluation gates, rather than through ad-hoc edits.
Cost accountability: explicit cost model documentation and budget ownership prevents 'no one knew it was that expensive' situations as traffic scales.
Model upgrade safety: the model version dependency log and upgrade approval workflow ensure that provider-side model changes are applied through a controlled, eval-gated process.
Trade-offs and limitations
PoC teams resist handoff overhead: teams under delivery pressure view documentation requirements as bureaucratic friction rather than risk management.
Documentation staleness: handoff documents must be maintained as the system evolves; a document that was accurate at handoff but never updated becomes worse than no document after six months.
Tacit knowledge loss: some PoC team knowledge — why a specific prompt structure was chosen, what experiments were tried and rejected — is hard to document and may be lost even with a formal handoff.
One-time transfer limitation: the handoff is a point-in-time event; ongoing communication structures between PoC and ops teams are needed to handle post-handoff questions.
How it works

The handoff package consists of two categories of artifacts: what the PoC team produces (six technical artifacts), and what the operations team must receive and confirm (six operational artifacts). The PoC team produces: the behavior specification and evaluation suite (linked, versioned, in the code repository); architecture decision records covering every major design choice and rejected alternative; a failure mode catalog listing the top 10 known failure modes observed during PoC and beta, with reproduction steps; a token cost model with scaling assumptions documented; the prompt change control process (how prompts are updated, who approves, and what eval gates must pass); and the model version and provider dependency log.

The operations team confirms receipt of: a runbook covering the top five failure modes with step-by-step remediation; an on-call escalation path that includes a 30-day support commitment from the PoC team; dashboard and alert configuration documentation; the model upgrade approval workflow; cost alert thresholds with a named budget owner; and the eval suite CI configuration so the operations team can run regression checks independently. The handoff is considered complete only when all items are confirmed by both parties and the PoC team lead and ops team lead have both signed the handoff document.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Handoff flow from PoC team artifacts to Platform/Ops team receipt, with sign-off gate and 30-day support commitment
attrs.title: Operational Handoff: PoC to Ops

content.text: The PoC team's six artifact categories are matched one-to-one with the six operational deliverables the ops team needs to take ownership. The arrow represents the formal handoff review, where both teams go through the checklist together. The 30-day support commitment by the PoC team after handoff provides a safety net for issues that surface only under production conditions.
When to use it

Start building handoff artifacts from day one of the PoC, not during the final sprint before launch. Architecture decision records written immediately after each design decision are 10x more accurate than those written retrospectively under deadline pressure. The formal handoff review should be scheduled at least three weeks before GA launch, leaving time to fill gaps identified during the review. A 30-day post-handoff support commitment from the PoC team should be negotiated at the start of the project, not when the handoff is requested.
How to implement
Create a handoff checklist at project kickoff and require each item to be filled in as the PoC progresses. Attach the checklist to the project tracker so it is visible in sprint reviews.
Write architecture decision records (ADRs) for every major design choice during development. Use a standard ADR template: context, decision, alternatives considered, consequences.
Maintain a failure mode catalog during beta: document every failure mode observed, its root cause, its frequency, and the remediation taken. This catalog becomes the basis for the ops runbook.
Schedule a joint PoC team + ops team walkthrough session three weeks before launch. Walk through each artifact together, not just hand them over — tacit knowledge is transferred in conversation.
Require the ops team to execute the runbook for at least two failure modes during the game day before signing off, confirming that the runbooks are executable by someone who did not write them.
Set up a 30-day post-handoff Slack channel where the PoC team answers ops team questions. Archive this channel: the questions and answers become supplementary documentation.
Examples
A handoff checklist template in YAML format:

# operational-handoff-checklist.yaml
# feature: invoice-extraction-agent
# PoC team lead: Priya M. | Ops team lead: James T.
# Handoff review date: 2025-10-20 | GA date: 2025-11-01

poc_team_artifacts:
  - id: PA-001
    name: "Behavior specification + eval suite"
    location: "repo: acme/invoice-agent/specs/ + tests/evals/"
    status: COMPLETE
    notes: "spec v2.1; 187 eval cases; 62 ACs. CI runs on every PR."

  - id: PA-002
    name: "Architecture Decision Records"
    location: "repo: acme/invoice-agent/docs/adrs/"
    status: COMPLETE
    notes: "7 ADRs covering: model choice, chunking strategy, fallback, PII filter, cost caching, context compression, eval judge model."

  - id: PA-003
    name: "Failure mode catalog"
    location: "repo: acme/invoice-agent/docs/failure-modes.md"
    status: COMPLETE
    notes: "10 failure modes documented. Top 3: rate limit exhaustion, context overflow on >12 page docs, PII in audit log gap."

  - id: PA-004
    name: "Token cost model + scaling assumptions"
    location: "repo: acme/invoice-agent/docs/cost-model.xlsx"
    status: COMPLETE
    notes: "Projections at 10k, 50k, 200k req/month. Assumes P95 input 8200 tokens."

  - id: PA-005
    name: "Prompt change control process"
    location: "repo: acme/invoice-agent/PROMPT-CHANGE-CONTROL.md"
    status: COMPLETE
    notes: "PR required; eval suite must pass at 92%; product lead + security lead approve."

  - id: PA-006
    name: "Model version + provider dependency log"
    location: "repo: acme/invoice-agent/docs/model-versions.md"
    status: COMPLETE
    notes: "claude-haiku-4-5 (primary), claude-sonnet-4-5 (fallback). Provider: Anthropic. Account: prod-api-01."

ops_team_artifacts:
  - id: OA-001
    name: "Runbook: top 5 failure modes"
    location: "wiki: acme/runbooks/invoice-extraction-agent"
    status: COMPLETE
    confirmed_by_ops: true
    game_day_executed: true

  - id: OA-002
    name: "On-call escalation path + 30-day PoC support"
    status: COMPLETE
    escalation_path: "OpsBot PagerDuty → AI-Ops-L1 → Priya M. (PoC lead, 30d support until 2025-12-01)"
    confirmed_by_ops: true

  - id: OA-003
    name: "Dashboard + alert configuration guide"
    location: "Datadog: acme/dashboards/invoice-agent-ops"
    status: COMPLETE
    confirmed_by_ops: true

  - id: OA-004
    name: "Model upgrade approval workflow"
    status: COMPLETE
    workflow: "Provider email → ops team assesses → eval suite re-run → product + security approve → staged rollout"
    confirmed_by_ops: true

  - id: OA-005
    name: "Cost alert thresholds + budget owner"
    status: COMPLETE
    budget_owner: "eng-director@acme.com"
    alert_threshold_usd: 18000  # monthly
    confirmed_by_ops: true

  - id: OA-006
    name: "Eval suite CI configuration"
    status: COMPLETE
    ci_system: "GitHub Actions"
    workflow_file: ".github/workflows/eval-suite.yml"
    confirmed_by_ops: true

sign_off:
  poc_team_lead: { name: "Priya M.", signed: true, date: "2025-10-20" }
  ops_team_lead: { name: "James T.", signed: true, date: "2025-10-20" }
  handoff_complete: true
An architecture decision record (ADR) template for AI-specific decisions:

# ADR-003: Primary Model Selection for Invoice Extraction Agent

## Status
Accepted (2025-08-12)

## Context
The invoice extraction agent must process 10,000–50,000 documents per month at a cost
below 8,000/month, with p95 extraction accuracy above 92%. We evaluated three model options.

## Decision
Use claude-haiku-4-5 as the primary model with claude-sonnet-4-5 as fallback.

## Alternatives Considered
1. **claude-sonnet-4-5 only**: Accuracy 96.2% (exceeds target), but cost 7,000/month
   at projected volume — 2.6x over budget. Rejected.
2. **claude-haiku-4-5 only**: Accuracy 93.1%, cost 3,200/month. Meets both targets.
   Accepted as primary.
3. **Open-source model (self-hosted)**: Accuracy 87.4% — below 92% target. Rejected.
   Infrastructure overhead also adds 2 FTE operational burden not budgeted.

## Evaluation Evidence
- Evaluated on 500-document test set sampled from production invoices
- Haiku: F1 92.3% for key field extraction, Sonnet: F1 95.8%
- Haiku latency p95: 4.2s, Sonnet latency p95: 9.8s (both within 12s SLA)

## Consequences
- **Positive**: Meets cost and quality targets; 3.6s faster p95 than Sonnet.
- **Negative**: 2.7% lower accuracy than Sonnet; may need upgrade if quality bar rises.
- **Risk**: If Anthropic deprecates claude-haiku-4-5, fallback to Sonnet will triple cost.
  Mitigation: review model availability quarterly; pre-negotiate pricing for Sonnet fallback.

## Owner
Priya M. (PoC Lead) | Review date: 2026-02-01
Real-world example

An insurance company built a claims triage AI assistant using a three-person PoC team over 10 weeks. At launch, the PoC team was redeployed to a new project with no formal handoff. The operations team that inherited the system had no documentation, no runbooks, and no understanding of why the system prompt was structured the way it was. Within four weeks: the ops team edited the system prompt to fix what they thought was a formatting issue, breaking the structured output format the downstream system expected; a model upgrade from Anthropic changed nuanced behavior in claim categorization, which no one detected for six weeks because there was no eval suite; and a context overflow on unusually long claims documents caused silent failures (empty responses) that were logged but not alerted on because no one had configured quality monitoring.

The total cost of remediating these three incidents — including the business cost of six weeks of miscategorized claims — exceeded the original PoC development budget. A handoff package with the six PoC artifacts and six ops artifacts would have prevented all three incidents. The prompt change would have been blocked by the prompt change control process; the model upgrade would have been caught by the eval suite re-run required by the upgrade approval workflow; and the context overflow alerts would have been configured from the dashboard guide received during handoff.
Common mistakes
Writing ADRs retrospectively under deadline pressure instead of immediately after each decision — retrospective ADRs lose the context and alternatives that make them valuable.
Handing over documents without a joint walkthrough session — documents transferred without conversation lose 70% of the tacit knowledge they contain.
Omitting the failure mode catalog because 'the PoC ran smoothly' — the most dangerous failure modes are the ones that did not appear during PoC but will at production scale or adversarial traffic.
Treating the 30-day support commitment as optional — without a structured post-handoff support period, the PoC team informally handles escalations indefinitely, preventing true knowledge transfer.
Allowing the ops team to sign off without executing at least two runbook scenarios during a game day — written confirmation of understanding is not the same as demonstrated operational competence.
Best practices
Build the handoff checklist into the project kickoff template so it is never treated as optional overhead added at the end.
Write ADRs immediately after each design decision using a standard template — the decision rationale degrades rapidly in human memory.
Conduct the handoff walkthrough as a joint session, not a document review — tacit knowledge transfers in conversation.
Require the ops team to execute at least two runbooks during the game day before signing the handoff document.
Set up a time-bounded (30-day) post-handoff support channel to capture and document the tribal knowledge that surfaces when the ops team first encounters production issues.
Performance and scalability

The operational handoff is a high-leverage investment relative to its cost. A thorough handoff takes 3–5 days of PoC team effort but prevents incidents whose remediation cost routinely exceeds 10–50 days of engineering time. The most scalable handoff programs treat the checklist as a living template that is improved after each project: post-incident reviews identify which handoff artifacts were missing or inadequate, and those gaps are added to the template for the next project.
Tooling and ecosystem
Tool
Purpose
Notes
Confluence / Notion
Runbook and ADR authoring
Use structured templates with version history
Git + Markdown (in repo)
ADRs co-located with code
Best for traceability; ADRs reviewed in PRs alongside code
PagerDuty
On-call and escalation
Configure during handoff, not after first incident
Jira / Linear
Handoff checklist tracking
Link checklist items to project tracker for sprint visibility
Comparison with alternatives
Approach
Knowledge transfer
Incident risk
Formal handoff checklist (this topic)
High — structured, verified, joint session
Low — runbooks, alerts, change control in place
Informal handoff (Slack messages)
Low — ad hoc, incomplete
High — undocumented failure modes, no runbooks
Same team operates PoC and production
N/A — no handoff needed
Medium — documentation still needed for resilience
Security and reliability

The prompt change control process in the handoff is a security control, not just an operational convenience. Without it, any operator with repository access can modify the system prompt without triggering the evaluation gate — creating the possibility of accidental or malicious behavioral regressions. The change control process ensures that prompt changes follow the same rigor as code changes: peer review, automated evaluation, and explicit approval from a named security reviewer before deployment.
Failure Mode Catalog Structure

The failure mode catalog is often the most valuable artifact in the handoff package because it documents the failure modes the PoC team discovered through direct experience — information that cannot be inferred from the architecture documentation alone. A well-structured catalog entry includes: the failure mode name, the trigger condition, the observed symptom, the impact if unaddressed, the diagnostic steps, the remediation, and the prevention. The following example shows how to structure the catalog and how the ops team uses it to generate runbooks.

import json
from dataclasses import dataclass, field, asdict
from typing import Optional
from enum import Enum
from pathlib import Path

class Severity(str, Enum):
    P1 = "P1"  # total outage
    P2 = "P2"  # degraded service
    P3 = "P3"  # minor degradation

@dataclass
class FailureMode:
    id: str
    name: str
    trigger_condition: str
    observed_symptom: str
    severity: Severity
    frequency_in_beta: str  # e.g., "3 times in 90 days"
    impact: str
    detection_signal: str   # what to look for in monitoring
    diagnostic_steps: list[str]
    remediation_steps: list[str]
    prevention: str
    escalation: str
    related_adr: Optional[str] = None

# Example failure mode catalog for invoice-extraction-agent
FAILURE_MODES = [
    FailureMode(
        id="FM-001",
        name="Rate Limit Exhaustion",
        trigger_condition=">45 concurrent requests sustained for >60 seconds",
        observed_symptom="429 responses from model API; requests queued or dropped",
        severity=Severity.P1,
        frequency_in_beta="2 times during earnings season peaks",
        impact="All invoice extraction fails; downstream AP processing blocked",
        detection_signal="Datadog: api_error_rate{status=429} > 0.05 for 2 minutes",
        diagnostic_steps=[
            "Check Anthropic dashboard for rate limit consumption",
            "Verify request queue depth in Redis queue-depth dashboard",
            "Identify which user/tenant is consuming disproportionate quota",
        ],
        remediation_steps=[
            "Enable request queue if not already active (ops runbook section 2.1)",
            "Apply per-tenant rate limiter if single tenant causing spike",
            "Escalate to Anthropic support if quota increase needed (SLA: 4h response)",
        ],
        prevention="Token bucket queue + per-user rate limiting (see ADR-005)",
        escalation="AI-Ops-L1 → Priya M. (PoC lead) if queue depth >1000 for >5 min",
        related_adr="ADR-005"
    ),
    FailureMode(
        id="FM-002",
        name="Context Overflow on Long Documents",
        trigger_condition="Invoice PDF >12 pages; input exceeds 10k token budget",
        observed_symptom="Empty extraction response or truncated JSON output",
        severity=Severity.P2,
        frequency_in_beta="18 times across 3 enterprise clients with complex invoices",
        impact="Invoice not processed; AP team must manually extract fields",
        detection_signal="Datadog: extraction_response_empty_rate > 0.02 for 10 minutes",
        diagnostic_steps=[
            "Check input_tokens in LLM trace for affected request IDs",
            "Retrieve the source document and count pages",
            "Verify chunking middleware was not bypassed (check request headers)",
        ],
        remediation_steps=[
            "Re-run with chunking explicitly enabled: POST /api/v1/analyze?force_chunk=true",
            "If re-run fails, escalate to manual processing queue (ops runbook 3.2)",
        ],
        prevention="Chunking middleware handles P99 document length; see ADR-003",
        escalation="P2: notify AP team lead; no after-hours page required",
        related_adr="ADR-003"
    ),
]

def generate_runbook_markdown(modes: list[FailureMode], output_path: str) -> None:
    """Generate an ops runbook Markdown file from the failure mode catalog."""
    lines = ["# Invoice Extraction Agent — Ops Runbook", ""]
    for fm in modes:
        lines += [
            f"## {fm.id}: {fm.name}",
            f"**Severity**: {fm.severity.value} | **Detection**: `{fm.detection_signal}`",
            "",
            f"**Trigger**: {fm.trigger_condition}",
            f"**Symptom**: {fm.observed_symptom}",
            "",
            "**Diagnostic steps**:",
        ]
        lines += [f"{i+1}. {s}" for i, s in enumerate(fm.diagnostic_steps)]
        lines += ["", "**Remediation steps**:"]
        lines += [f"{i+1}. {s}" for i, s in enumerate(fm.remediation_steps)]
        lines += ["", f"**Escalation**: {fm.escalation}", ""]
    Path(output_path).write_text("\n".join(lines))
    print(f"Runbook written to {output_path}")

generate_runbook_markdown(FAILURE_MODES, "/tmp/runbook.md")

This approach — maintaining failure modes as structured data objects and generating runbooks programmatically — keeps the catalog and the runbook in sync. When a new failure mode is discovered in production and added to the catalog, the runbook is regenerated automatically. The structured format also enables querying: the ops team can filter by severity, frequency, or affected component to prioritize which runbooks to train on first.
Key takeaways
The operational handoff requires two sets of artifacts: six produced by the PoC team and six confirmed received by the ops team — all twelve must be complete before sign-off.
ADRs must be written immediately after each design decision, not retrospectively — retrospective documentation loses the alternatives and context that make ADRs valuable.
The joint walkthrough session transfers tacit knowledge that documents alone cannot capture — make it a required part of the handoff process, not an optional meeting.
The 30-day post-handoff support commitment by the PoC team should be negotiated at project kickoff and treated as a project deliverable, not a favor.
The prompt change control process is a security control: prompt changes without an evaluation gate are a source of silent behavioral regressions.
The cost of a thorough handoff (3–5 PoC team days) is typically 1–5% of the cost of the first major production incident that a missing handoff would have caused.
